text: laparoscopic robotic-assisted Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and hiatal hernia repair. A sixty-six-year-old woman with type II diabetes mellitus, GERD, esophagitis, hiatal hernia, dumping syndrome after cholecystectomy and severe obesity with a BMI of forty kilograms square meters.
The gastroscopy reveals the presence of a hiatal hernia, with no visible signs of H. pylori infection.

The patient is positioned with open legs for the laparoscopic robotic-assisted Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and hiatal hernia repair. The surgical assistant is positioned between the patient's legs, and the trocars are visibly placed in the abdominal area as depicted.

Inside the abdominal cavity, a large hiatal hernia is visible. The surgeon uses graspers to reduce the herniated stomach and begins dissection of the right crus. A latex band is applied for esophageal traction to facilitate the reduction of the abdominal esophagus.

The surgeon uses a hook instrument for precise dissection of the mediastinum, taking advantage of its full robotic articulation. The hiatus is closed with Effibon stitches, which are calibrated for accuracy.

The surgeon dissects the lesser curvature of the stomach and uses a tri-stapler with a 60mm suture and preloaded mattress material to horizontally create the gastric pouch. A 36 French bougie is used for calibration, followed by the vertical knitting of two 60mm sutures.

The surgeon performs a side-to-side anastomosis between a 60 cm biliopancreatic limb and a 150 cm alimentary Roux limb using a 60 mm tanned cartridge. The gastrojejunum anastomosis is completed in a hand-sewn terminal lateral fashion, with two planes of PDS running sutures visible.
No drains were used. The patient was discharged at day two after surgery. GERD symptoms resolved.
